description: Practical knowledge about solar panel cleaning, panel soiling, and environmental conditions affecting solar systems. Includes residential, commercial, industrial, and agricultural environments common on the California Central Coast.

why_panels_get_dirty.summary: Solar panels accumulate dust, pollen, bird droppings, and airborne particles that reduce system efficiency.

rain_myth.summary: Rain does not clean solar panels.
key_points: Rain rinses loose dust but does not remove the dirt film bonded to solar glass.
Bird droppings, pollen, and sticky residues remain on the panels after rain.
As rainwater dries it often leaves behind mineral residue patterns.
rain_myth.approved_customer_answer: Rain does not clean solar panels. It rinses loose dust but leaves behind the dirt film, pollen, and bird droppings that reduce system efficiency.

soiling_effects.summary: Soiling reduces solar production and causes a measurable drop in system efficiency.
key_points: Dust buildup blocks sunlight from reaching the solar cells.
Bird droppings create shaded areas that limit panel output.
Uneven dirt patterns cause sections of the array to produce less power.

general_guidance: Most residential systems benefit from cleaning every 6 to 12 months.
Dust heavy environments require more frequent cleaning.
Large commercial arrays benefit from scheduled maintenance programs.

central_coast_environment.summary: The Central Coast produces strong solar panel soiling conditions due to agriculture and coastal weather patterns.
factors: agricultural soil dust from surrounding fields
seasonal pollen from crops and trees
marine layer residue that dries onto solar glass
wind carrying fine soil particles across rooftops
central_coast_environment.impact_statement: Solar systems located in agricultural regions accumulate dust faster than typical suburban installations.

commercial_and_industrial_solar.summary: Commercial solar installations operate on a much larger scale than residential systems and require structured maintenance to maintain production.
system_scale: Commercial arrays often contain hundreds or thousands of panels.
Energy production from these systems offsets large operational electricity costs.
soiling_characteristics: Large flat rooftops collect dust and debris quickly.
Industrial activity releases airborne particulates that settle on panels.
Bird activity increases around large rooftop structures.
maintenance_value: Cleaning restores solar production across large arrays.
Even small efficiency losses across hundreds of panels result in significant energy loss.
Routine maintenance protects long term system performance.

agricultural_commercial_solar.summary: Solar systems located in agricultural environments accumulate dust faster than almost any other type of installation.
environmental_conditions: constant airborne soil dust from surrounding farm fields
tractor and equipment movement stirring up soil particles
wind carrying fine agricultural dust onto rooftops
harvest activity dramatically increasing dust levels
panel_soiling_behavior: fine soil dust forms a uniform film across the solar glass
dust accumulation increases rapidly during planting and harvest seasons
large exposed arrays collect dust from every direction

central_coast_ag_example.description: Produce cooling and packing facilities commonly sit in the middle of active agricultural fields and typically install large rooftop solar systems.
key_points: Cooling facilities operate refrigeration systems with heavy electrical demand.
Solar arrays offset a large portion of that electricity consumption.
Dust from surrounding farmland settles continuously onto the solar panels.

maintenance_importance: Dust buildup directly reduces energy production across large arrays.
Agricultural facilities benefit from more frequent solar panel cleaning.
Routine cleaning supports consistent production during peak harvest operations.

safe_cleaning_principles.summary: Solar panels require cleaning methods designed specifically for solar glass and electrical equipment.
best_practices: Use purified water where spotting is a concern.
Avoid direct high pressure spraying on solar panels.

customer_education_snippets.text: Rain rinses loose dust but it does not remove the dirt film, pollen, and bird droppings that reduce solar production.
Solar panels located near agricultural fields collect fine soil dust that forms a film across the glass surface.
Large commercial solar systems contain hundreds or thousands of panels, so even small efficiency losses add up quickly.
